lines.text: Section eight. E. chromi — University of Cambridge, 2009.

Cambridge's 2009 team won the Grand Prize with E. chromi — engineered E. coli that grow in vivid colours, plus a set of regulatory parts called sensitivity tuners that control when the colours are produced.
The motivation was both pragmatic and aesthetic.
Pragmatic: biosensors built with synthetic biology need accessible readouts.
Measuring GFP fluorescence requires fluorescence detection equipment — fine for a laboratory, useless for a field worker in a rural village trying to test drinking water.
Coloured bacteria produce a visible signal that any human can interpret without instruments.
Aesthetic: nature produces an enormous diversity of pigments, and bringing some of that diversity into E. coli expands the visual palette of what engineered biology can produce.
Cambridge produced pigment-producing parts in several colours.
Orange and red: production of carotene and lycopene, using the carotenoid biosynthesis pathway whose four enzymes were already available as BioBricks from earlier iGEM teams.
Black or brown: production of melanin from tyrosine, using a melA gene from Rhizobium etli, which the team adapted from prior published work into a usable BioBricks part via PCR.
Purple and green: production of violacein and its derivative deoxyviolacein, using a five-gene operon from the naturally purple bacterium Chromobacterium violaceum.

The five-gene operon was directly synthesised by gene synthesis — the team did not have a natural source they could clone from — and was designed to allow easy modification: removing one gene converts the operon from purple violacein production to green deoxyviolacein production.
The pigment biology is striking but the engineering rigour is more striking.

Beyond just producing colours, the team built sensitivity tuner parts — regulatory elements that filter biosensor inputs and only produce the coloured pigment when the input crosses a meaningful threshold.
This addresses a fundamental biosensor design problem: how do you ensure your sensor responds to real signals and not to background noise?
Sensitivity tuners that can be combined with any biosensor and pigment output give the field a building block for designing readable, threshold-gated biosensors.

The team imagined applications: a dipstick of engineered E. chromi cells dropped into a water sample, producing different colours depending on which contaminants are present — heavy metals, pathogens, agricultural pollutants.
Different combinations of sensitivity tuners and pigments would produce different colour signatures for different contaminants, all readable by eye.
Beyond the laboratory work, the team partnered with two professional designers to explore how the technology could shape future applications.
The collaboration produced concept art and design fiction around the pigment-producing biosensors.

An art installation called E. chromi — based on the engineered bacterial cultures — has since been nominated for major art and design awards, demonstrating that synthetic biology can productively cross over into the arts and humanities.
Cambridge's project illustrated something important about iGEM: real laboratory work plus rigorous characterisation plus thoughtful outreach is what wins the Grand Prize.
